title: Behavioral Symptoms of Dementia: Nonpharmacologic Comfort First
subtitle: An 82-year-old with advanced Alzheimer's in memory care is agitated, wandering, and sundowning. The nurse must look for the unmet need behind the behavior, use calming person-centered measures first, and avoid restraints and antipsychotics.

patient.name: R. Whitaker
patient.admitDate: Memory care resident with advanced Alzheimer's. Worsening agitation, wandering, door-seeking, and late-day (sundowning) restlessness over recent weeks. Goals are comfort, dignity, and avoiding restraints and hospital transfers.

vitals.temp: Dim light, music, validation; no PRN

labs.v: Check UTI, constipation, dehydration, pain, meds.
Acknowledge the feeling, not the facts.

nursesNote: R. Whitaker, 82, has advanced Alzheimer's and shows worsening behavioral and psychological symptoms of dementia (BPSD) — agitation, wandering, door-seeking, and late-day sundowning. These behaviors are treated as communication of an unmet need, so the nurse looks first for physical causes: when agitation peaks mid-morning, toileting yields 250 mL with visible relief, and a workup (clean UA, PAINAD 1, a bowel movement the prior day, medication review) rules out a delirium trigger such as UTI, constipation, pain, or dehydration. Nonpharmacologic, person-centered comfort is first-line: a consistent routine and familiar caregivers, a calm voice and redirection, validation therapy (acknowledging her feeling rather than correcting facts), reminiscence about her teaching career, and her preferred music. As the sundowning window approaches, the environment is prepared with dim lighting, reduced stimulation, a familiar quilt, and an early supper, and the episode resolves without any as-needed medication. Antipsychotics are avoided given the FDA black-box warning for increased death and stroke in older adults with dementia (reserved, low-dose and brief, only if there is risk of harm and nonpharmacologic measures fail), restraints are avoided, and the team partners with the daughter on comfort-focused goals (no tube feeding in advanced dementia).
providerOrders: Assess for unmet needs and rule out delirium (toileting, pain, UTI, constipation, hydration, meds)
Use nonpharmacologic, person-centered comfort measures first (routine, validation, calm environment)
Prepare the environment for sundowning (dim lighting, reduced stimulation, soothing routine)
Avoid restraints; reserve low-dose antipsychotics only for risk of harm when nonpharm fails, then reassess
Partner with family on comfort-focused goals of care

opts.text: Agitation, wandering, and sundowning are common behavioral symptoms of dementia
These behaviors often signal an unmet need (pain, toileting, hunger, environment)
A sudden change in behavior should prompt a check for delirium (UTI, constipation, pain)
Nonpharmacologic comfort measures are first-line
Agitation in dementia should be treated first with an antipsychotic
A calm voice, redirection, and meeting needs can de-escalate without medication
The DICE approach (Describe, Investigate, Create, Evaluate) structures the workup
Antipsychotics carry a black-box warning for increased death and stroke in elderly dementia
Validation therapy acknowledges the patient's feeling rather than correcting facts
Physical restraints increase injury and agitation and are avoided
Tube feeding improves outcomes in advanced dementia
Sundowning is eased by dim lighting, reduced stimulation, and soothing routines

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the response to an agitation episode

opts.text: Ensure safety for the patient and others
Check for and meet physical needs (toilet, pain, hunger, thirst)
Use calm redirection, validation, and a soothing environment
Investigate for delirium causes (UTI, constipation, medications)
Reserve low-dose medication only if there is risk of harm and nonpharm fails
Offer toileting and check for pain when agitation begins
Use a calm voice, reminiscence, and familiar music
Rule out a UTI or constipation as a trigger
Dim the lights and reduce noise as sundowning approaches
Apply restraints to stop the wandering
Give an antipsychotic as the first response to agitation
Argue with the patient to correct her confusion
Start tube feeding for the weight loss of advanced dementia

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Maintain a consistent routine and familiar caregivers
Meet physical needs proactively (toileting, hydration, comfort, pain)
Provide calming activities (music, reminiscence, family contact)
Prepare the environment for sundowning (dim light, low stimulation)
Evaluate the response and partner with family on comfort goals
Agitation resolves after toileting and a calm, familiar routine
The nurse uses validation and music instead of an as-needed sedative
A delirium workup (UA, bowel check, medication review) is completed
The team plans comfort-focused care with the family
The patient is placed in restraints to keep her in her room
An antipsychotic is given routinely before trying nonpharmacologic measures
Staff repeatedly correct the patient, increasing her distress
A feeding tube is placed for advanced-dementia weight loss
